text:
"We're shipping AI so customers get more value."

You hear it in every roadmap review right now. It's the line PMs use to defend a 20-30% price bump on the next tier. It feels true because the features are real, the demos are slick, and the board is asking what the AI story is.

Then you look at how those features get used.

Across the portfolios I've seen, the AI capabilities buyers paid a premium for sit at single-digit weekly active usage six months in. IT leaders signed the contract. Employees ignored the feature. The autonomous workflows nobody trusts the agent to run still get done in a spreadsheet.

So the value story breaks down. Customers bought a price increase wrapped in a feature they're not ready to operationalize. The renewal conversation in 2026 is going to be brutal for vendors who can't show usage tied to outcomes.

The honest version: most AI pricing right now is a valuation play, not a value play. The 40%+ premium AI-native companies trade at is real, and execs want a piece of it. Fair. But you can't extract that premium from customers forever without adoption catching up.

The shift worth making: stop measuring AI features by attach rate to higher tiers. Measure them by whether a human stopped doing the work.

If your AI feature didn't remove a task, you sold a price increase.

What's your usage number on the AI feature you charged more for?

#ProductManagement #AIProduct #B2BSaaS #AIStrategy #SaaS #ProductStrategy
topic: AI features in B2B SaaS products
